cataloging_subject.label: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Frederick J. Furnivall's editorially created 1882 EETS selection and arrangement of 51 numbered English testamentary units dated 1387–1439 and 1454. The cataloging subject is not one medieval authored work, not one manuscript or probate register, and not necessarily 51 independent physical documents. Individual testators and testamentary instruments, seven aggregate registered-copy witnesses, the 1882 edition, OUP's 1964 reprint, the Google/Internet Archive scan, U-M's 1999 encoding, and the checked-in XML remain distinct descriptive and lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London, A.D. 1387–1439; with a Priest's of 1454
catalog_summary.author: Fifty-one named testators represented by registered testamentary copies
catalog_summary.date: Testamentary units 1387–1439 and 1454; edited selection first published 1882
catalog_summary.region: England, especially London and southern England; one selected codicil made at Rouen

titles.value: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London, A.D. 1387–1439; with a Priest's of 1454

agents.name: Fifty-one named testators
notes: A cataloging collective for the persons whose testamentary acts Furnivall numbered 1–51; it is not a claim of joint authorship, common institutional identity, or uniform documentary responsibility.

The edition sometimes presents a will with codicil or related testamentary instrument, so item-level roles should distinguish testator, declarant, scribe, witness, executor, registrar, and probate official where evidence permits.

agents.name: Frederick James Furnivall
notes: Compiler, transcriber, editor, and principal commentator of the 1882 EETS volume.

agents.name: J. Challenor Smith
notes: Probate registry official who identified the earliest English wills for Furnivall, assisted with difficult readings, and supplied additional register information and a Latin-will note.

scope.description: Testamentary declarations and dispositions represented in the 51 numbered units

notes: The schema role author is used broadly for documentary creation. A registered copy may preserve a testator's act through a scribe or registrar rather than autograph composition.

scope.description: Selection, chronological ordering, numbering, framing, and assembly of the 1882 edition-level compilation
The 1882 EETS edition and its 1964 reprint
English wills and related register entries copied from the Commissary Court and PCC registered-copy volumes

notes: The edition's phrase 'original registers' identifies the register volumes Furnivall consulted; PROB 11 records are registered copies, not necessarily testators' signed originals.

scope.description: Dedication, forewords, corrections, headings, notes, and indexing apparatus

notes: The XML omits both concluding indexes but retains the other identified framing and commentary.

scope.description: Source identification, assistance with difficult readings, and additional register-derived notes

assertion.notes: The schema's collator role approximates several kinds of source assistance described by Furnivall; it does not imply a formal critical collation of every text.

scope.description: Dated testamentary acts represented in the 51 numbered edition units

notes: The title's continuous 1387–1439 range is followed by an explicitly additional priest's will of 1454; it must not be normalized as an unbroken 1387–1454 run without qualification.

scope.description: Furnivall's transcription, selection, arrangement, correction, and annotation of the compilation
First publication of Furnivall's EETS edited selection

notes: Place where Stephen Thomas's codicil, part of edition unit 15, is reported as made.

Location of both probate jurisdictions' registers as consulted by Furnivall and residence or activity of many selected testators

notes: London is an important archival and social center for the collection, not the place of origin of every selected testamentary act.

Primary vernacular language of the selected testamentary texts

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Testamentary and probate formulae, embedded clauses, citations, and an editorially quoted antiphon

notes: The XML header's Latin-only language declaration is incomplete and is not propagated as whole-work language metadata.

language_statements.label: English

scope.description: Furnivall's title, framing, headings, glosses, notes, translations, corrections, and indexes

notes: Modern editorial English is distinct from the Middle English documentary text.

form_statements.description: A prose edited documentary collection of wills, testaments, codicils, probate language, and editorial apparatus. Verse excerpts in the forewords and endnotes are ancillary editorial quotations, not the substantive form of the testamentary units.

scope.description: Fifty-one numbered testamentary edition units, some including codicils or related instruments
Testamentary dispositions as preserved through ecclesiastical probate registration

subject_statements.label: Religious bequests and late medieval piety
Household goods, clothing, books, and material culture
Late medieval London and English social history

summaries.value: Furnivall's EETS volume assembles 51 numbered English testamentary units from the late fourteenth and early fifteenth centuries, chiefly wills dated 1387–1439 with an additional priest's will of 1454. Drawn from registered copies in three Commissary Court of London registers and four Prerogative Court of Canterbury register units, the texts document inheritance, landholding, household goods, clothing, books, religious bequests, burial, charity, occupations, family relations, and urban and regional networks. Furnivall framed the transcriptions with a dedication, polemical and linguistic forewords, corrections, source-register reference lists, extensive notes, and two indexes.

The 1882 edition contains title and imprint matter; a contents table; dedication to J. Challenor Smith; forewords; a figure division; four pages of Additions and Corrections; 51 consecutively numbered testamentary units on main-text pp. 1–134; lists of other English wills in the Luffenam and Rous registers; endnotes on pp. 135–143; a List of Names and Places on pp. 145–169; and a List of Words and Subjects on pp. 170–200. The U-M XML represents all stated material except the two concluding indexes and retains the duplicated Arabic pagination of corrections and main text.

This record describes Furnivall's 1882 edition-level edited selection. Its 51 numbers are editorial units, not a uniform count of original wills, registered entries, codicils, or physical documents. The source witnesses are seven aggregate probate register units whose many unselected entries remain outside the work. The U-M header's Church of England institutional author heading describes probate provenance and is not treated as authorship by the Prerogative Court, especially because 27 selected units derive from the Commissary Court of London registers. Item-level creators, scribes, documentary boundaries, and original-versus-registered-copy relationships require complete source-image inspection.

extent.description: The cataloged testamentary collection contains 51 numbered prose edition units. The XML has 58 DIV1 containers: five front-matter divisions, 51 consecutive will-unit divisions, one register-reference division, and one endnotes division; it also has 16 DIV2 and 3 DIV3 containers. Raw XML counts include 54 L elements, but these occur in ancillary editorial quotations and a Latin antiphon with English translation in the notes, so they are excluded from the substantive will-form metrics. The XML's 51 units are not a physical-document count.

content_parts.label: Title matter, contents, dedication, forewords, figure, and Additions and Corrections
content_parts.summary: Edition framing that explains Furnivall's selection and transcription circumstances, surveys themes and language, acknowledges contributors and limitations, and supplies four separately paginated pages of corrections.

locators.value: title matter; contents pp. v–vii; dedication pp. viii–x; forewords pp. xi–xv; Additions and Corrections pp. 1–4

notes: Verse quoted from Handlyng Synne in the forewords is editorial evidence and does not change the testamentary collection's prose form.

content_parts.label: Fifty-one numbered testamentary units
content_parts.summary: Chronologically arranged registered testamentary texts dated 1387–1439 and 1454, including wills and some associated codicils or testamentary supplements from seven probate register units.
notes: Source distribution is Courtney 2, Broun 5, More 20, Rous 2, Marche 2A 1, Marche 2B 10, and Luffenam 11; these child counts total 51.
The numbering remains an edition structure. Complete register images are required before equating a number with one entry, one testamentary act, or one physical document.

content_parts.label: References to other English wills in Luffenam and Rous
content_parts.summary: Edition lists or notes additional English-language register entries that Furnivall did not transcribe as numbered units in this volume.
notes: These references do not enlarge the numbered collection's item count.

content_parts.label: Editorial notes
content_parts.summary: Furnivall's and contributors' corrections, identifications, linguistic observations, historical commentary, quotations, and notice of the first known bequest of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales.
notes: An embedded note contains a Latin Salve Regina and modern English translation encoded in verse lines; this is editorial quotation, not a testamentary unit.

content_parts.label: List of Names and Places and List of Words and Subjects
content_parts.summary: The edition's two concluding indexes, preserved in the complete 1882 scan but deliberately omitted from the U-M electronic encoding and repository XML.

locators.value: List of Names and Places pp. 145–169; List of Words and Subjects pp. 170–200

notes: This part belongs to the cataloged 1882 edition but not to the repository representation's encoded content.

evidence.summary: Supplies encoded title and identifier, U-M and 1964 edition statements, legacy rights text, content structure, all 51 numbered will units, register milestones and folio labels, languages visible in the text, framing, and omission declaration for the two indexes.

evidence.citation: Element, attribute, and source-structure inspection of repository EEWills.xml.
evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, DIV3, P, L, PB, MILESTONE, FOREIGN, NOTE1, BIBL, TYPE, and NODE
evidence.summary: Counts 58 DIV1, 16 DIV2, 3 DIV3, 295 P, 54 L, 161 PB, 84 MILESTONE, 115 FOREIGN, 395 NOTE1, and 137 BIBL elements; confirms 51 consecutive edition will units and locates the verse lines in editorial rather than substantive testamentary contexts.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, EEWills item record and full text.
evidence.summary: Confirms the public electronic item, stable identifier, inherited author heading, Furnivall editorship, 1964 source edition, current table of contents, and present public-domain/NoC-US assertion.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London, EETS Original Series 78 (1882).
evidence.summary: The complete cached edition supports title, editor and contributors, selection rationale, transcription history, publication, contents, testators, dates, places, languages, themes, source-register names and folios, editorial practices, notes, and indexes.

evidence.citation: Visual and technical inspection of the complete cached 1882 Google/Internet Archive PDF.
evidence.summary: Confirms complete 1882 bound-volume coverage through printed p. 200, including both indexes omitted from XML, and confirms that the scan is not a 1964 manifestation.

evidence.citation: The London Archives catalogue, DL/C/B/004/MS09171/001–003, Courtney, Broun, and More registers.
evidence.summary: Confirms exact current references, register names and date ranges, former reference MS 09171, Latin and English, directed microfilm use, original-access restrictions, and More's unfit-for-consultation condition.

evidence.citation: The National Archives, Discovery records for PROB 11/1, 11/2A, 11/2B, and 11/3.
evidence.summary: Confirms exact PCC register references, names, date and quire ranges, holdings and public-record status, and selected item-level reference mappings.

evidence.citation: The National Archives, Wills 1384–1858 research guide.
evidence.summary: Establishes that PROB 11 records are registered copies, not original wills, and describes item-level digital access.

evidence.citation: Layer-specific U-M, Internet Archive, repository-license, TLA, and TNA rights evidence recorded in the EEWills lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Supports preserving current U-M NoC-US language, the restrictive 1999 XML notice, repository software licensing, 1882 edition status, scan provider terms, and archive-image terms as separate claims.

evidence.citation: Dated complete-surrogate search for the 1964 reprint and seven probate register witnesses.
evidence.summary: No public complete scan of the exact 1964 reprint or any complete register witness was found; TLA directs use of microfilm, and TNA exposes item-level downloads plus a reproduction route.

open_questions.question: How do the 51 numbered editorial units map to individual wills, testaments, codicils, probate acts, register entries, and any surviving original testamentary documents?
next_steps: Inspect complete source images for all selected register entries and record document boundaries, entry structure, language, named roles, foliation, endorsements, and relationships among instruments.
Search separately for surviving original wills; preserve registered copies, original instruments, and Furnivall's edition numbers as distinct identifiers and layers.
Build a many-to-many crosswalk rather than forcing 51 one-to-one document records.

open_questions.question: What are the exact item-level creators, scribes, documentary roles, dates, languages, and dialectal features for each testamentary instrument?
next_steps: Describe testator, declarant, scribe, registrar, probate official, witnesses, executors, and beneficiaries separately from complete source images.
Do not propagate the collective testator label, Middle English, London, or the U-M Prerogative Court author heading to every component without item evidence.
Distinguish original dialect from London register-copy spelling, following Furnivall's warning that copyists altered dialect.

open_questions.question: What exact material or textual differences distinguish the 1964 OUP reprint from the 1882 first edition used to describe the corpus work?
next_steps: Inspect a complete physical or scanned 1964 copy and compare every page, including title leaf, verso, preliminaries, corrections, main text, notes, indexes, and colophon, with the cached 1882 scan and U-M milestones.
Do not cite the cached 1882 PDF as a manifestation-level facsimile of 1964.

open_questions.question: Which terms control redistribution of the repository EEWills XML given current U-M NoC-US language, the restrictive embedded 1999 notice, and a repository Unlicense introduced as applying to software?
next_steps: Obtain written clarification from U-M on whether the 1999 availability statement has been superseded for bulk XML reuse.
Obtain a repository-maintainer statement defining the Unlicense's scope for imported XML data and encoded texts.
Continue to separate text, edition, encoding, markup, scan, and archive-image rights.

notes: The XML's Church of England, Province of Canterbury, Prerogative Court institutional author heading is not used as the work author: 27 of the 51 selected units derive from the Commissary Court of London registers, and probate custody does not equal authorship.
The title's 'Fifty' is retained as title information; the edition and XML number 51 units because Nicholas Sturgeon's 1454 priest's will is appended after the 1387–1439 sequence.

Courtney, Broun, More, Rous, Marche 2A, Marche 2B, and Luffenam are aggregate registered-copy witnesses containing many unselected entries; complete-object requests must not be limited to Furnivall's folios.

Current TLA records direct users to microfilm and mark all three originals unavailable for general access; More is also unfit for consultation. The record does not represent the original registers as normally orderable onsite.
No public complete register facsimile or exact 1964 reprint scan was located as of 2026-07-14; the cached 1882 scan is complete for that edition and includes the two indexes omitted by U-M.
